channel.title: NYT > U.S. > Politics
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: U.S. Solar Installations Fell in 2025 as Trump Attacked Clean Energy
item.description: More solar energy was added to U.S. grids than any other technology, but the amount installed fell by 14 percent, according to a new report.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.media:credit: Mason Trinca for The New York Times
item.media:description: A solar field in Riesel, Texas, in 2023. Solar power is projected to account for just over half of the new power projects that will be added this year.

item.title: Trump Seeks to Calm Oil and Gas Markets but Says Iran War Will Go On
item.description: The president said the U.S. could accompany tankers through the Strait of Hormuz if necessary to keep oil flowing. “We’ve already won in many ways, but we haven’t won enough,” he said.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Oil tankers sat anchored in Muscat, Oman, on Saturday as Iran vowed to block the Strait of Hormuz amid the conflict.

item.title: Two Supreme Court Justices Debate Handling of Trump Emergency Cases
item.description: In a rare joint appearance, Justices Ketanji Brown Jackson and Brett M. Kavanaugh offered sharply different views on how the court should handle emergency requests.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: Supreme Court Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson, left, moderator Judge Paul L. Friedman, center, and Supreme Court Justice Brett M. Kavanaugh on Monday, where the two justices differed over how the court is handling emergency requests.

item.title: Trump’s War in Iran, and Rising Gas Prices, Collide With Midterm Agenda
item.description: The attack on Iran has led to a surge in energy prices at a moment when the cost of living is a major issue heading into the fall elections.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A tanker and a cargo ship in Muscat, Oman, on Monday. The war in Iran has affected the shipping of oil through the Strait of Hormuz and the Gulf of Oman.

item.title: Trump Again Suggests Without Evidence That Iran Struck Elementary School
item.description: After a video showed a Tomahawk missile hitting a naval base next to the school, President Trump claimed Iran possesses such weapons. But the U.S. developed the missile and has sold it only to a few close allies.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh and John Ismay
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The president suggested without giving evidence on Monday that Iran or another country could have fired the weapon.

item.title: Iran’s Women’s Soccer Team Offered Asylum by President Trump
item.description: The dissonance between the president’s hard-line immigration policies and his offer of asylum to the athletes was striking. Australia took in five players.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.media:credit: Dave Hunt/Australian Associated Press, via Reuters
item.media:description: Members of the Iranian women’s soccer team after a match on Australia’s Gold Coast on Sunday.

item.title: Oil Prices and Stocks Swing Wildly as Iran and Israel Launch New Strikes
item.description: Oil prices and stocks initially swung wildly, and Iran’s foreign minister promised “surprises,” calling the strikes against his country “Operation Epic Mistake.”
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman, Farnaz Fassihi, Richard Pérez-Peña and Thomas Fuller
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: People in Tehran showing their support on Monday for Mojtaba Khamenei, who was selected as Iran’s new supreme leader.

item.title: Iran War Overshadows Midterms as Democrats Focus on Economy
item.description: Democrats are focused on the economy, while Republicans wish they were focused on the economy.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: An explosion after an airstrike in Tehran on Saturday.

item.title: 2 Teen Mariachi Musicians Released From ICE Detention
item.description: Representative Joaquin Castro of Texas led a delegation of Democrats to a South Texas detention center to press for the release of the brothers and their family.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Joaquin Castro, Democrat of Texas, greeting Caleb Gámez-Cuéllar and his family after their release from the Dilley Immigration Processing Center in Dilley, Texas, on Monday.

item.title: U.S. Reaches Tentative Deal Ending Prosecution of Turkish Bank
item.description: Court papers submitted as part of the agreement with Halkbank, which was accused of doing business with Iranian entities, said Turkey’s assistance “was instrumental” in the Israel-Hamas cease-fire.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:description: President Recep Tayyip Erdogan of Turkey has long pressed President Trump to quash the case against the Turkish bank.

item.title: Alexander Butterfield, Who Revealed Nixon Tapes in Watergate Scandal, Dies at 99
item.description: “There is tape in the Oval Office,” said Mr. Butterfield, a former White House aide, in testimony that rocked the Watergate hearings and led to the president’s resignation.
item.dc:creator: Robert D. McFadden
item.media:description: Alexander P. Butterfield testifying before the Senate Watergate Committee in July 1973. He revealed to the American public the existence of a secret recording system that President Richard M. Nixon had authorized and that had picked up virtually all of his meetings and telephone conversations.

item.title: Judge Halts Trump Administration Move to Restrict Immigration Appeals
item.description: The ruling is part of a broader dispute between the independent federal judiciary and the executive branch’s immigration court system.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague and Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:description: 26 Federal Plaza in Manhattan, where detained immigrants attend their court hearings.

item.title: As Kari Lake Sought to Shutter Voice of America, Parent Agency Rebuffed Auditors
item.description: A new report says the lack of information provided for a required annual examination was so “pervasive” that auditors declined to opine on the agency’s financial numbers.
item.dc:creator: Minho Kim
item.category: United States Agency for Global Media
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kari Lake has vowed to shut down federally funded newsrooms that she accused of being anti-American and “rotten.”

item.title: G7 Countries Hold Off on Releasing Emergency Oil Supplies
item.description: Finance ministers for the seven industrialized countries met on Monday and said they would consider releasing oil from reserves but were not ready to do so now.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Aurelien Breeden
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: Smoke rose from a refinery operated by Bapco Energies, Bahrain’s state-owned energy company, on Monday. Global oil prices have spiked since the U.S.-led war with Iran began.

item.title: New Lawsuit Challenges Rubio’s Threats Against Foreign Tech Regulators
item.description: A lawsuit filed on Monday argues that a State Department’s decision to withhold visas from experts who have pushed for stronger social media regulations is illegal.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio announced last year that he was restricting visas from foreign nationals whose calls for global content moderation policies, he said, amounted to “flagrant censorship actions.”

item.title: Trump Threatens to Crowd Out Republicans’ Midterm Message
item.description: As the G.O.P. gathered in Miami for a party retreat where lawmakers hoped to focus on the economy, the president threatened to block his own party’s legislative agenda.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Polls suggest that Republicans’ chances of keeping control of the House are growing more slim.

item.title: How Markwayne Mullin Became Trump’s Homeland Security Pick
item.description: The Oklahoma senator whom President Trump chose to run the Department of Homeland Security has risen in Washington by styling himself as a MAGA warrior and Trump loyalist.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: If Mr. Mullin were to be confirmed by the Senate, he would take the mantle of a federal agency carrying out the president’s immigration agenda.

item.title: James Talarico Challenges ‘Heretical’ Right-Wing Christianity in Texas Senate Race
item.description: James Talarico, the Democratic nominee from Texas, hopes to counter what he sees as a conservative takeover of the American church.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer and Elizabeth Dias
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Talarico has cast his Senate candidacy as an opportunity for Christians to reclaim what he sees as core principals of their faith in American political life.

item.title: U.S. Carries Out Another Boat Strike, Killing Six
item.description: The attack, in the eastern Pacific, was part of a continuing campaign by the U.S. Southern Command to target people suspected of smuggling drugs by sea.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )

item.title: White House Removes Republican Member of N.T.S.B.
item.description: J. Todd Inman, who was prominent in the investigation of the midair collision in Washington last year, said no reason was given for his firing two years into his term on the transportation safety board.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: J. Todd Inman of the National Transportation Safety Board during a Jan. 27 hearing on the midair collision that killed 67 people near Reagan National Airport the previous January.
